content:
4 min read
Just now

--

I used to make this decision the same way most people do: open three pricing pages, find the cheapest plan that didn’t look obviously crippled, and go with that. I don’t do that anymore, and it’s not because I got smarter about reading pricing pages. It’s because I watched the same mistake play out across enough teams to stop trusting the sticker price at all.
Here’s the thing nobody tells you when you’re comparing AI coding tools for the first time: the price on the page is the least informative number on it. The numbers that actually predict whether you’ll be happy in six months are how predictable your spend stays under real usage, how much cleanup the code needs after the demo, and whether you can actually explain what got built. I’ve started calling these credits, quality, and control, mostly because it’s easier to remember than “the three ways this decision usually goes wrong.”
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Credits: the part that gets you when you’re not looking
I don’t think most people who picked an AI coding tool in early 2026 realized how unstable the pricing underneath them actually was. Within the same month, three of the biggest names in this space, GitHub Copilot, Cursor, and Windsurf restructured how they charge for usage, independently and almost simultaneously, according to Digital Applied’s breakdown of the change. If you’d budgeted around the old model, you found out about the new one when your bill did something you didn’t expect.
My honest opinion: that instability isn’t a one-time event you wait out. It’s the new normal for a market where the underlying model costs keep shifting and vendors keep re-pricing around it. So I stopped asking “what does this cost per month” and started asking “what does this cost in a bad week” a week where the team is heads-down, burning through agent sessions, pushing right up against whatever the limit is. If a tool can’t answer that clearly, that’s the answer.
Quality: the cost that shows up later, not now
This is the one that actually changed my mind the most. A study of more than 300,000 AI-authored commits across thousands of production repositories found that over 15% of commits from every major coding assistant introduced at least one new code-quality issue and nearly a quarter of those issues were still sitting there, unresolved, months after they shipped, per the research on arXiv. That’s not a tooling problem you catch in a demo. It’s a problem that shows up the first time someone tries to change that code and realizes nobody, including the person who “wrote” it, actually understands what it’s doing.
I’ve started treating “it passed the demo” as roughly meaningless. The real question is whether the code that got generated has tests that mean something, an architecture someone can describe out loud, and a structure that won’t fight back the next time a feature needs to touch it. That’s a much less exciting thing to optimize for than “ship it in five minutes,” but it’s the difference between a tool I’d trust with a real product and one I’d only trust with a weekend project.
Control: the question I didn’t think to ask until I’d been burned
Control is the one people skip, and it’s the one that’s bitten the hardest. In 2025, a misconfigured database permission issue on a popular vibe-coding platform left roughly one in ten analyzed deployments exposing personal data, financial records, and hardcoded API keys to anyone who knew where to look, as documented by Janea Systems in their breakdown of the incident. That’s not a hypothetical “could happen” risk. It happened, at scale, to real applications that real people were using.
What stuck with me about that story wasn’t the bug itself, bugs happen. It was that the teams affected often didn’t know enough about what their own tool had generated to catch it before it shipped. That’s control, stripped down to its simplest form: do you actually know what was built, or did you just trust that it worked because it looked finished? I’d genuinely rather move a little slower and know the answer to that question than move fast and find out the hard way, the way those teams did.
There’s a real cost to finding out the hard way, too. An analysis cited by BuildMVPFast put the number of startups that built production apps primarily with AI tools and later needed full or partial rebuilds in the thousands, at $50,000 to $500,000 each. That’s not a rounding error. That’s a startup’s entire seed round, spent twice, because nobody checked under the hood the first time.
What I actually do now
I run something close to the credits-quality-control check on anything new before I let it touch a real product:

• Credits — Can I predict the bad-week cost, not just the advertised price?

• Quality — Does the output come with tests and an architecture I can explain, or just a working demo?

• Control — Can I actually see what decisions got made, and would I be comfortable defending them in a security review?
This isn’t me saying every tool fails this test, and it’s definitely not me saying there’s one tool that wins it outright, the landscape is genuinely split by design, with some platforms optimized for speed and others, like 8080.ai, built around documenting architecture and logging agent decisions before code ships, which leans harder into the quality and control side of the equation. I’ve used tools across that whole spectrum, and the right one still depends on what you’re building and what stage you’re at.
What I won’t do anymore is pick based on the number on the pricing page. That number was never the real cost. It just took me a few expensive lessons to believe that.
